explanation: The natural language describes a transition condition from the MANEUVER state to the STANDBY state. Specifically, it states that if we are in the MANEUVER state (state_is_MANEUVER) and the pilot is in control (standby) and sensor data is good (good), then the autopilot shall change states from MANEUVER to STANDBY.  This can be expressed with the G operator to represent that this must hold globally, and the U operator to represent that the state must change eventually.

The natural language states that if the pilot is in control of the vehicle (standby) and sensor data is good, then the autopilot changes from MANEUVER (state_is_MANEUVER) to STANDBY (state_is_STANDBY). This means globally, if standby and good hold, then eventually state_is_STANDBY will hold after state_is_MANEUVER.

The natural language states that if the pilot is in control (standby) and sensor data is good, then the autopilot must transition from MANEUVER to STANDBY. This can be expressed in LTL as a global property. G(state_is_MANEUVER & standby & good -> X state_is_STANDBY)

The natural language can be expressed in LTL by using the 'until' operator to specify that the autopilot will change from MANEUVER to STANDBY until the pilot is in control and the sensor data is good. The atomic proposition 'state_is_MANEUVER' indicates that the autopilot is in MANEUVER mode, 'state_is_STANDBY' indicates that the autopilot is in STANDBY mode, 'standby' indicates that the pilot is in control of the vehicle and 'good' indicates that the sensor data is good. The formula captures the requirement that autopilot shall change states from MANEUVER to STANDBY, once the condition of 'pilot is in control and sensor data is good' occurs.

The natural language states that when the pilot is in control (standby) and sensor data is good, then autopilot internal state transitions from MANEUVER to STANDBY. This can be captured by an implication: if standby & good, then next state_is_STANDBY should be true. Further, state_is_MANEUVER should be false in the next state.

The natural language states that if the pilot is in control and sensor data is good (standby & good) and the autopilot is in the MANEUVER state (state_is_MANEUVER), then eventually the autopilot shall change states to STANDBY (state_is_STANDBY).

The natural language indicates a state transition upon conditions being met, from MANEUVER to STANDBY. 'state_is_MANEUVER' is true and when the pilot is in control ('standby') and the sensor data is good ('good'), the system will transition to 'state_is_STANDBY'.

The natural language sentence 'The autopilot shall change states from MANEUVER to STANDBY when the pilot is in control (standby) and sensor data is good' translates to the following LTL formula: G(state_is_MANEUVER & standby & good -> X state_is_STANDBY). It means that globally, if the current state is MANEUVER, the pilot is in control (standby) and sensor data is good, then in the next state the state must be STANDBY.

The natural language states that the autopilot must change from MANEUVER to STANDBY, when the pilot is in control (standby) and sensor data is good. We represent 'the autopilot shall change states from MANEUVER to STANDBY' with 'state_is_MANEUVER -> X state_is_STANDBY'. We combine this with the condition 'when the pilot is in control (standby) and sensor data is good' which is equivalent to 'standby & good' so the final LTL statement is 'G((state_is_MANEUVER & standby & good) -> X state_is_STANDBY)'

The LTL formula 'G((standby & good & state_is_MANEUVER) -> X(state_is_STANDBY))' captures the requirement that if the pilot is in control (standby), sensor data is good, and the autopilot is in the MANEUVER state, then in the next state, the autopilot shall change to the STANDBY state. 'G' ensures this holds globally.

The natural language states that the autopilot state changes from MANEUVER to STANDBY when pilot is in control (standby) and sensor data is good. In LTL, we can represent this as: if the autopilot is currently in the MANEUVER state, and the pilot is in control (standby), and the sensor data is good, then in the next state, the autopilot will be in the STANDBY state.

The natural language states that if the pilot is in control (standby) and the sensor data is good, then the autopilot must change from MANEUVER to STANDBY state. This can be captured using an implication where the antecedent is 'standby & good' and the consequent is that if the current state is MANEUVER then the next state must be STANDBY. This translates to G((standby & good & state_is_MANEUVER) -> X(state_is_STANDBY))

The natural language says that when 'standby' and 'good' are true, the autopilot changes from MANEUVER to STANDBY. This is captured by the LTL formula: G((state_is_MANEUVER & standby & good) -> X(state_is_STANDBY)).

The natural language states that eventually the system changes from MANEUVER to STANDBY. This change happens when the pilot is in control (standby) and sensor data is good. Thus, when the state is MANEUVER, eventually (F) the state will become STANDBY, only when standby and good are true.

The natural language statement says that if the autopilot is in the MANEUVER state (state_is_MANEUVER), the pilot is in control (standby) and sensor data is good (good), then the next state of the autopilot will be STANDBY (state_is_STANDBY).

The LTL formula 'G((state_is_MANEUVER & standby & good) -> X(state_is_STANDBY))' captures the requirement that if the autopilot is in MANEUVER state, the pilot is in control (standby), and sensor data is good, then in the next state, the autopilot should be in STANDBY state. The 'G' operator ensures that this condition holds globally, i.e., at all times.

The natural language states that the system will transition from state_is_MANEUVER to state_is_STANDBY when standby and good are true. This is captured by the LTL formula G((state_is_MANEUVER & standby & good) -> X(state_is_STANDBY)). It states that globally, if the current state is MANEUVER and standby and good are true, then in the next state, the state will be STANDBY.
